Based on the search results, here's what I found about Raycast extension API components and developer documentation:

## Official Documentation

The primary resource is available at https://developers.raycast.com, where you can get started with the Raycast API.

## Core Technologies

Extensions are built with TypeScript, React, and Node. Developers use React to declare the UI through custom Raycast components, and can use React hooks for state management, which may cause re-rendering of the user interface.

## UI Components

Built-in UI components are available to help developers be consistent with all extensions. The API includes several high-level components, including:

- List - to show multiple similar items, such as a list of open todos
- Grid - similar to a List but with more legroom to show an image for each item, such as a collection of icons

The Grid component is designed for laying out media-heavy information like icons, images or colors, and allows you to layout differently sized items with an API designed close to the List component for smooth adoption.

## Developer Experience

The API offers a strongly typed interface, hot-reloading, and modern tooling. npm run dev starts the extension in development mode with hot reloading, error reporting and more.

For detailed API reference documentation, component specifications, and tutorials, visit the official Raycast developers website directly.

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of Raycast's desktop app UI and screens:

## Core Interface

Raycast is activated with a keyboard shortcut (Option+Space by default on Mac, or Alt+Space on Windows) and displays a window at the center of the screen, awaiting the user's input and displaying a list of suggested apps. Raycast uses incremental search as the user types to predict the action the user intends to perform and display the corresponding result.

## UI Output Formats

The application outputs the data in different formats such as lists and grids with a single or two-pane layout, supporting various data types like text with Markdown, media, and additional input fields.

## Main Feature Screens/Views

The desktop app includes several key screens and views:

1. **Window Management**: Raycast's Window Manager extension allows you to quickly snap windows to various positions and sizes using customizable shortcuts or intuitive drag-and-drop.

2. **Calculator**: Raycast's built-in calculator is a frequently used feature that allows you to open Raycast, type in an expression, and see the result immediately, with the Enter key even copying the answer to the clipboard.

3. **File Search**: File Search easily locates your documents and shows recently opened files, making file management a breeze.

4. **Clipboard History**: Raycast's clipboard history is searchable and offers a nice view of copied images.

5. **AI Chat**: Raycast AI is a built-in feature (Pro plan) that lets you chat with AI models like GPT-4 and Claude directly from the launcher, allowing users to ask questions, explain code, draft emails, and more.

6. **Snippets & Text Expansion**: You can create snippets and search for them and paste them in any app or set trigger keywords that will auto-expand the text.

## Technology

Raycast uses React for its user interface declaration and renders the supported elements to its native UI, with a set of UI components available for building extensions.

# ActionPanel Sub-Components and Built-in Action Types

## ActionPanel Sub-Components

Based on the documentation, ActionPanel has the following sub-components:

1. **ActionPanel.Section** - "A group of visually separated items" used to organize related actions within a panel.

2. **ActionPanel.Submenu** - A specialized action that "replaces the current ActionPanel with its children when selected," useful for presenting options like labels or assignees.

## Built-in Action Types

The documentation references Action types through examples but doesn't provide a comprehensive list of all built-in actions. The examples shown include:

- `Action.OpenInBrowser` - Opens a URL in the default browser
- `Action.CopyToClipboard` - Copies content to the clipboard
- `Action` - A generic action component with customizable `onAction` callbacks

**Note:** The documentation excerpt provided doesn't contain a complete enumeration of all available built-in Action types. For a full list, you would need to consult the Actions API reference page directly.

# Form Sub-Components and Input Types

The Raycast Form component includes the following sub-components and input types:

## Text Input Components
- **Form.TextField** - Standard text input field
- **Form.PasswordField** - Secure password entry field
- **Form.TextArea** - Multi-line text input with optional markdown support

## Selection Components
- **Form.Checkbox** - Boolean toggle input
- **Form.Dropdown** - Single-selection dropdown menu with optional sections
- **Form.TagPicker** - Multi-selection tag picker

## Date/Time Component
- **Form.DatePicker** - Date and datetime selection with min/max constraints

## File System Component
- **Form.FilePicker** - File and directory selection with single/multiple modes

## Layout Components
- **Form.Separator** - Visual divider for grouping form items
- **Form.LinkAccessory** - Optional link in the search bar

## Key Features
Each component supports both controlled and uncontrolled modes. They feature customizable validation through error messages, optional info tooltips, and value persistence via the `storeValue` prop. The Form itself manages drafts to preserve incomplete submissions and includes an `isLoading` state indicator.

# List Component and Sub-Components Summary

## Main Components

**List** - Container displaying sections or items with built-in filtering, search, and pagination support.

**List.Item** - Individual entity in the list with title, optional subtitle, icon, and accessories.

**List.Section** - Visually grouped collection of related items with optional title and subtitle.

## Detail Components

**List.Item.Detail** - Right-side panel showing markdown content and/or metadata for selected items.

**List.Item.Detail.Metadata** - Structured data display at the bottom of detail view.

**List.Item.Detail.Metadata.Label** - Title with optional icon and text value.

**List.Item.Detail.Metadata.Link** - Clickable link element within metadata.

**List.Item.Detail.Metadata.TagList** - Horizontal row of colored tags.

**List.Item.Detail.Metadata.TagList.Item** - Individual tag with optional icon and text.

**List.Item.Detail.Metadata.Separator** - Visual divider for grouping metadata items.

## Search & Filter Components

**List.Dropdown** - Accessory dropdown menu in search bar for secondary filtering.

**List.Dropdown.Item** - Individual dropdown option with title and value.

**List.Dropdown.Section** - Grouped dropdown items with section header.

## Utility Components

**List.EmptyView** - Friendly message displayed when no items match search or list is empty, featuring optional icon and description.

## Key Props Reference

Common props include: `filtering`, `isLoading`, `isShowingDetail`, `navigationTitle`, `onSearchTextChange`, `pagination`, `searchBarAccessory`, `searchText`, and `selectedItemId`.

content.content:
# Built-in Action Types

Here are all the built-in Action types available in the Raycast API:

1. **Action** - "A context-specific action that can be performed by the user"

2. **Action.CopyToClipboard** - Copies content to clipboard and displays a confirmation HUD

3. **Action.Open** - Opens a file or folder with a specific application

4. **Action.OpenInBrowser** - "Action that opens a URL in the default browser"

5. **Action.OpenWith** - Opens a file/URL with user-selected application from submenu

6. **Action.Paste** - "Pastes the content to the front-most applications"

7. **Action.Push** - "Pushes a new view to the navigation stack"

8. **Action.ShowInFinder** - "Shows a file or folder in the Finder"

9. **Action.SubmitForm** - "Adds a submit handler for capturing form values"

10. **Action.Trash** - "Moves a file or folder to the Trash"

11. **Action.CreateSnippet** - Navigates to Create Snippet command with prefilled fields

12. **Action.CreateQuicklink** - Navigates to Create Quicklink command with prefilled fields

13. **Action.ToggleQuickLook** - "Toggles the Quick Look to preview a file"

14. **Action.PickDate** - "Action to pick a date"

# Navigation Patterns in Raycast

## Core Concept

Raycast implements a **stack-based navigation model** where view components are pushed onto and popped from a navigation stack. This allows users to navigate between different screens in a command interface.

## useNavigation Hook

The `useNavigation` hook is the primary tool for programmatic navigation. According to the documentation, it's described as "A hook that lets you push and pop view components in the navigation stack." However, the guide notes you'll typically use the Push Action component instead for most use cases.

## Push and Pop Operations

The Navigation object returned by the hook provides two key methods:

**Push**: Adds a new component to the stack with the signature `(component: React.ReactNode, onPop: () => void) => void`. This transitions users to a new view.

**Pop**: Removes the current component from the stack, returning users to the previous view. The system automatically triggers a pop when users press the ESC key.

## Practical Usage Pattern

The documentation illustrates a simple toggle pattern: a "Ping" component pushes a "Pong" component onto the stack, which can then pop back to the original view. This demonstrates how components can manage their own navigation flow through the provided methods.

This stack-based approach provides intuitive back-button behavior while maintaining a clear component hierarchy throughout the user's interaction with a command.

I found the Raycast API documentation for feedback components. Here's what the search results show:

## Toast, Alert, and HUD Components

The Raycast API provides three main feedback UI components:

**Toast:**
Use Toast.Style.Success for confirmations and Toast.Style.Animated when your Toast should be shown until a process is completed. Toasts can have actions associated with them, for example to provide a way to cancel an asynchronous operation, undo an action, or copy the stack trace of an error. The showToast() will fallback to showHUD() if the Raycast window is closed.

**Alert:**
You can ask for confirmation by using confirmAlert when the user takes an important action like when irreversibly deleting something. Use Alert.ActionStyle.Default for confirmations of a positive action and Alert.ActionStyle.Destructive for confirmations of a destructive action like deleting a file.

**HUD:**
When the user takes an action that closes Raycast (for example copying something), you can use a HUD to confirm the action worked properly—it will automatically hide the main window and show a compact message at the bottom of the screen.

You can access the complete API documentation at:
- **Toast**: https://developers.raycast.com/api-reference/feedback/toast
- **Alert**: https://developers.raycast.com/api-reference/feedback/alert
- **HUD**: https://developers.raycast.com/api-reference/feedback/hud

content.content:
# Alert Component Overview

## confirmAlert Function

The `confirmAlert` function creates and displays a confirmation dialog. It accepts an `Alert.Options` object and returns a Promise resolving to a boolean—`true` for the primary action, `false` for dismissal.

**Usage pattern:**
```typescript
if (await confirmAlert({ title: "Are you sure?" })) {
  // Handle confirmation
}
```

## Alert.Options Properties

The configuration object supports:
- **title** (required): Displayed below the icon
- **message**: Additional details, useful for destructive action confirmations
- **icon**: Visual illustration for the alert
- **primaryAction**: Main user action
- **dismissAction**: Cancellation behavior
- **rememberUserChoice**: Adds a "Do not show again" checkbox that persists user preference

## Alert.ActionOptions

Each action requires:
- **title** (required): Action label
- **onAction**: Optional callback when triggered
- **style**: Visual styling via `Alert.ActionStyle`

## Alert.ActionStyle Variants

Three styling options exist:

1. **Default** — For positive confirmations
2. **Destructive** — For irreversible actions like deletion
3. **Cancel** — For cancellation actions

These styles provide visual cues matching the action's consequence severity, helping users understand the impact before proceeding.

# Toast Component Overview

## Purpose
The Toast component notifies users about asynchronous operations and errors. It can include actions like canceling operations, undoing actions, or copying error details. If the Raycast window closes, `showToast()` "will fallback to showHUD()"

## Styles (Toast.Style)
Three visual styles are available:
- **Animated**: Displays while a process runs; can be updated or hidden later
- **Success**: For confirmations and successful operations
- **Failure**: For displaying errors

## showToast Function Signature
```typescript
async function showToast(options: Toast.Options): Promise<Toast>
```
Returns a Promise resolving to a Toast object that can be modified or hidden afterward.

## Key Properties
**Toast.Options** (required):
- `title` (required): Main heading
- `message` (optional): Additional details
- `style` (optional): Visual appearance
- `primaryAction` and `secondaryAction` (optional): User interactions

**Toast object** supports:
- `show()`: Display the Toast
- `hide()`: Conceal the Toast
- Property updates for dynamic changes

## Actions (Toast.ActionOptions)
- `title` (required): Action label
- `onAction` (required): Callback receiving the Toast instance
- `shortcut` (optional): Keyboard shortcut binding

# Grid Component Overview

## Sub-Components

The Grid component includes the following sub-components:

- **Grid.Section** - Groups related grid items together with optional title and subtitle
- **Grid.Item** - Individual grid cell displaying content (image, emoji, or color)
- **Grid.Dropdown** - Filtering dropdown appearing in the search bar
- **Grid.Dropdown.Section** - Groups dropdown items visually
- **Grid.Dropdown.Item** - Individual dropdown menu option
- **Grid.EmptyView** - Displayed when no items are available

## Column/Item Size Options

Column configuration uses numeric values (1-8), replacing the deprecated `Grid.ItemSize` enum:
- **1 column** - Single wide layout
- **5 columns** - Medium density (replaced "Medium" size)
- **8 columns** - High density (replaced "Small" size)

The deprecated `Grid.ItemSize` enumeration included Small, Medium, and Large options, but numeric column counts now provide greater flexibility.

## Inset Options

The `Grid.Inset` enum controls spacing between item content and borders:

| Option | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| **Small** | Minimal spacing |
| **Medium** | Moderate spacing |
| **Large** | Maximum spacing |

## Content Fit Options

The `Grid.Fit` enum determines how content scales:

- **Contain** - Preserves aspect ratio with letterboxing if needed
- **Fill** - Scales to fill the cell (may crop content)

Based on my search results, here's comprehensive information about Raycast's built-in commands and features:

## Core Built-in Features

Raycast is a productivity launcher for Mac that lets you quickly open apps, run commands, manage windows, use AI, and automate tasks—all from your keyboard. It replaces Spotlight and combines multiple tools (clipboard manager, window manager, text expander) into one app.

### Clipboard History
Raycast added a new Paste Sequentially command that allows you to paste previously copied content one after another into your frontmost application. Clipboard History is a searchable history where you can find that specific code snippet, API key, or URL you copied hours ago without scrolling endlessly.

### Snippets
Snippets allow you to define keywords to expand into boilerplate code, common commands, email templates, or anything you type repeatedly. It provides a fuzzy-search interface for snippets and inline expansion from a trigger phrase in any text field, with dynamic placeholders like the current date or the most recent clipboard.

### Quicklinks
Raycast can store links as titled quicklinks that show up in the main search and can have dynamic placeholders. You can open frequently used paths, files, and URLs faster with Raycast's Quicklinks extension, creating shortcuts to launch anything: links in the browser, project folders in the terminal, or web searches.

### File Search
File Search Beta was updated to be on parity with Windows File Search.

### Spotlight Replacement
Raycast is a fast app and file launcher that replaces or augments Spotlight with a keyboard-centric command palette. You can disable Spotlight's CMD+Space shortcut in System Settings and set CMD+Space as the Raycast hotkey.

### Other Built-in Tools
Built-in tools include a calculator, clipboard history, emoji picker, notes, and window management in the same interface.

## Recent Updates (2024-2025)

At the end of 2024, Raycast Pro mainly unlocks AI features that let you chat with an LLM from Raycast or translate text, plus cloud settings sync and more advanced window management commands.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Raycast's UI design system and search bar:

## Search Bar Design

The search bar is at the center of Raycast, with almost every interaction going through it. The bar was made bigger to reflect its importance, grab the user's attention and set it apart from the search results below.

## UI Design System Components

Raycast uses React for its user interface declaration and renders the supported elements to native UI. The API comes with a set of UI components that you can use to build extensions, functioning as a design system. The high-level components include List (to show multiple similar items), Grid (similar to a List but with more legroom for images), Detail (to present more information), and Form (to create new content).

## Visual Improvements

The team collaborated with Designer James McDonald to overhaul icons with an outline style and bolder stroke width to make them more prominent. The size of the leading icon of the search results was increased, making it quicker to scan for what you need to find.

# Raycast Commands and Core Features

Based on the manual page for Dynamic Placeholders, here are the key features described:

## Dynamic Placeholders

The manual documents placeholder functionality for making quicklinks, snippets, and AI commands dynamic. Key placeholders include:

- **Clipboard Text**: `{clipboard}` - "Inserts your last copied text"
- **Snippets**: `{snippet name="…"}` - References other snippet content
- **Cursor Position**: `{cursor}` - Moves cursor when pasted into apps
- **Date/Time**: `{date}`, `{time}`, `{datetime}`, `{day}` - Insert temporal data
- **UUID**: `{uuid}` - "Inserts a universally unique value"
- **Selected Text**: `{selection}` - Pulls from frontmost application
- **Arguments**: `{argument}` - User input with optional names and defaults
- **Browser Tab**: `{browser-tab}` - Content from focused tab (requires extension)

## Modifiers and Formatting

The manual describes several text transformation modifiers: uppercase, lowercase, trim, percent-encode, and json-stringify. Users can also apply date offsets and custom date formats using syntax like `{date format="yyyy-MM-dd"}`.

## Additional Features

- Clipboard history offset support
- Browser tab format options (markdown, text, HTML)
- CSS selectors for precise content extraction
- Argument options for predefined choices

# Raycast App Design Refresh: Visual Changes

## Key Design Updates

The Raycast team implemented five major visual refinements to enhance usability while maintaining their core design philosophy of "fast, simple, and delightful."

### 1. **Enlarged Search Bar & Icons**
The primary search interface received prominence through increased sizing to reflect its central role. Icon dimensions in search results were also expanded for "quicker to scan for what you need to find."

### 2. **New Action Bar Architecture**
A consolidated bottom panel replaced scattered UI elements. This action bar displays:
- Left side: Navigation title and toast notifications (previously hidden)
- Right side: Available actions with keyboard shortcuts visible for quick learning

The redesign makes the previously obscured ⌘K command discovery feature more prominent.

### 3. **Icon System Overhaul**
Designer James McDonald created a unified icon set using consistent stroke widths and corner radii. The system employs "an outline style, and a bolder stroke width to make them more prominent," creating visual cohesion across the application.

### 4. **Compact Mode**
A focused interface option minimizes non-essential elements, optimizing for the typical interaction flow of launching, searching, and selecting results.

### 5. **Updated App Icon**
Replacing the outdated flat design, the new icon features a keycap metaphor symbolizing keyboard accessibility. The design drew inspiration from Apple's Magic Keyboard aesthetic and supports alternative icon variants.

# MenuBarExtra Component Overview

## Core Concept

The `MenuBarExtra` component enables developers to create menu bar items in macOS that appear in the system menu bar's extras section. As the documentation states: "The `MenuBarExtra` component can be used to create commands which populate the extras section of macOS' menu bar."

## Visual Structure

Menu bar commands operate through a hierarchical UI system:

- **MenuBarExtra** (root): The container displaying an icon and/or title in the menu bar
- **MenuBarExtra.Item**: Individual clickable menu entries with optional icons and subtitles
- **MenuBarExtra.Submenu**: Nested menus that group related items
- **MenuBarExtra.Section**: Logical groupings with optional titles and automatic separators

## How It Works

When a user clicks the menu bar icon, Raycast loads the command, executes it, and keeps it in memory while the menu remains open. The component can return `null` to remove items from the menu bar or a `MenuBarExtra` element to display content.

## Key Lifecycle Events

Menu bar commands activate through five scenarios:
1. Direct execution from Raycast's root search
2. Scheduled background refresh intervals
3. User clicking the menu bar icon/title
4. Raycast application restart
5. Re-enabling the command in preferences

## Configuration

Commands require `"mode": "menu-bar"` in their `package.json` entry and can include optional `"interval"` values for automatic background refresh.

# Raycast Settings/Preferences Tabs

Based on the documentation, Raycast has four main preferences tabs:

1. **General** - Allows customization of core functionality, including launch at login and replacing Apple's Spotlight as the primary search tool.

2. **Appearance** - Lets users select between light and dark themes (with system appearance as default) and adjust text size options.

3. **Extensions** - Enables configuration of all available extensions, including enabling/disabling them and their commands. Users can "log in to Jira or disable our signature for new issues" and set aliases and hotkeys.

4. **Advanced** - Provides additional settings for display positioning, timing before returning to root search, and the ability to "export your Raycast preferences, and import them to a new device" for setup efficiency.

5. **About** - Displays version information, links to the website, feedback options, and acknowledgements.

# Raycast Terminology and UI Mapping

## Core Concepts

**Action**
- Definition: "Little functionality to control something" like adding labels or copying links
- Visual Element: Accessible through the Action Panel interface

**Action Panel**
- Definition: Interface "located on the bottom right" that displays available actions
- Visual Element: Bottom-right UI component; opened via `⌘` `K`

**Command**
- Definition: "A type of entry point for an extension" that appears in root search
- Visual Element: Searchable entries supporting scripts, lists, forms, and complex UI

**Extension**
- Definition: "Add functionality to Raycast" consisting of commands and installable components
- Visual Element: Discoverable and installable packages from the Store

**Manifest**
- Definition: The `package.json` file containing "npm package mixed with Raycast specific metadata"
- Visual Element: Configuration file (not a visible UI element)

**Tool**
- Definition: AI-focused entry points that "don't show up in the root search" but enable AI interactions
- Visual Element: Backend functionality accessible through AI mentions (@mentions in Quick AI, Commands, Chat)

**AI Extensions**
- Definition: Regular extensions enhanced with tools, allowing "@mention" integration with AI
- Visual Element: Extended extensions accessible through AI chat interfaces

# Raycast Icon & Image Reference

## Icon Enum Values

The `Icon` enumeration provides numerous built-in icons for Raycast actions and list items. The content shows a comprehensive table of icon names organized alphabetically, including:

**Sample Icon Values:**
- Navigation: `ArrowUp`, `ArrowDown`, `ArrowLeft`, `ArrowRight`, `ChevronUp`, `ChevronDown`
- UI Controls: `Check`, `Checkmark`, `Circle`, `Dot`, `Ellipsis`
- Common Actions: `Download`, `Upload`, `Copy`, `Paste`, `Delete`, `Duplicate`
- Objects: `Document`, `Folder`, `Calendar`, `Clock`, `Gear`, `Settings`
- Status: `Battery`, `Signal`, `Cloud`, `Bolt`
- Miscellaneous: `Gift`, `Flag`, `Star`, `Heart`, `Lock`, `Key`

## Usage Pattern

The documentation provides this example:

```typescript
import { Icon, List } from "@raycast/api";

export default function Command() {
  return (
    <List>
      <List.Item title="Icon" icon={Icon.Circle} />
    </List>
  );
}
```

Icons are passed as the `icon` prop to UI components like `List.Item`, using the enumerated constant format (e.g., `Icon.Circle`).

## Note on Images & Colors

The provided content focuses exclusively on icon enumeration members and doesn't include dedicated sections covering Image types or Color options, so additional documentation would be needed for those features.

# Raycast Manual Sections

Based on the web page content provided, the Raycast Manual is organized into the following main sections:

## Platform Guides
- **Raycast Mac Manual** - The primary desktop application for macOS
- **Raycast Windows Beta Manual** - Windows version (currently in beta)
- **Raycast iOS Manual** - Mobile application for iOS devices

## Community & Development
- **Community Guidelines** - Standards for user conduct
- **Extensions Guidelines** - Resources for building custom extensions
- **Script Commands** - Information about creating automation scripts

- **Raycast AI Privacy + Security** - Details about data handling for AI features
- **Acceptable Use Policy** - Terms governing platform usage

## Frequently Asked Questions
The manual addresses common inquiries including:
- Local application and file search capabilities
- Multi-account support for integrated services
- Extension update procedures
- Corporate licensing availability
- International accessibility

## Community Channels
The manual directs users to connect via Slack, X (Twitter), and Instagram for additional support and community engagement.

# Raycast Window & Search Bar API Functions

Based on the provided documentation, here are the available functions:

## 1. **clearSearchBar**
- **Parameters:** `options?: { forceScrollToTop?: boolean }`
- **Purpose:** "Clear the text in the search bar"

## 2. **closeMainWindow**
- **Parameters:** `options?: { clearRootSearch?: boolean; popToRootType?: PopToRootType }`
- **Purpose:** Closes the main Raycast window with control over search clearing and pop-to-root behavior

## 3. **popToRoot**
- **Parameters:** `options?: { clearSearchBar?: boolean }`
- **Purpose:** "Pops the navigation stack back to root search"

---

**Note:** The documentation provided does not include `getSelectedText` or `getWindowState` functions. Only the three functions listed above are documented in the supplied content.

# Search Bar Component Anatomy

Based on the Raycast Manual content, here's an overview of the search bar's structure:

## Core Features

**Focus & Input**: The search bar is "focused by default to make it quick to start typing your query," enabling rapid command access.

**Search Capability**: The interface supports fuzzy search functionality, allowing users to match content by typing letters independently of their position. For example, "jci" finds Jira Create Issue and "msg" locates Messages.

## Placeholder Text & Hints

The documentation notes that you can "Check the Search Bar placeholder in commands to see what you can search for." This suggests placeholders provide contextual hints about searchable content within specific views.

## Root Search Functionality

In the main/root search context, the fuzzy algorithm is configurable through settings. Users can adjust sensitivity via **Raycast Settings > Advanced > Root Search Sensitivity** to control result precision:

- **High sensitivity**: Stricter matching, requiring greater precision
- **Low sensitivity**: More inclusive results matching typed letters

## View-Specific Implementation

The search bar adapts across different contexts—root search for applications and commands, and content-specific search within pages—though the page content doesn't detail distinct anatomical differences across view types or mention dedicated filter modes or dropdown accessories.

# Raycast AI Views Overview

## Quick AI
Quick AI provides rapid answers without opening a separate window. Users type questions directly into Raycast and press Tab to access this feature. It's designed for "one-off questions" rather than extended conversations. The interface shows immediate AI responses within the main Raycast window.

## AI Chat
A dedicated chat window functions as a persistent assistant throughout the workday. Users search for "AI Chat" to launch it, then engage in multi-turn conversations. The window displays message history, allowing follow-up questions and context retention. Users can apply AI Commands to previous messages using Cmd+K shortcuts.

## AI Commands
These are customizable prompts for specific tasks. Built-in examples include "Explain This in Simple Terms" and "Improve Writing." Users select text and invoke commands via Raycast search. Custom commands can include dynamic placeholders like `{selection}` or `{argument}`, enabling flexible, template-based operations. Commands can be duplicated and personalized with custom instructions and creativity settings.

## Model Selection
Users choose AI models from a picker interface available in settings and chat. The system supports various providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google. A "Manage Models" command lets users enable/disable specific models. Models display capability badges (like AI Extensions support) on their cards.

## Additional Features
AI Extensions allow "@-mention" functionality to invoke extension capabilities within conversations. Image generation through DALL·E and Stable Diffusion integrates seamlessly into the chat experience.

The chat includes pinned conversations, custom presets tailored to specific tasks, and the ability to compare model responses mid-conversation.

**Quick AI**: A lightweight, unobtrusive overlay accessed via a single hotkey, appearing as a "floating window above your other apps." This mode enables rapid queries with optional web search integration for current information with inline citations.

**Chat Presets**: Customizable chat environments that let users select preferred models and configure system instructions. Users can switch between preset conversations (TypeScript Expert, Swift Assistant, Cars Expert, etc.) for specialized tasks.

**Attachment Support**: The Mac app integrates OS-level access to PDFs, CSV files, and screen content, bringing contextual information directly into conversations without manual copying.

**Model Comparison**: Mid-chat functionality allowing users to regenerate responses using different models to determine the most suitable output for their needs.

**Web Integration**: Optional real-time search capability providing "up-to-date information with inline references," enhancing response accuracy for current events and recent data.

The interface emphasizes accessibility through keyboard shortcuts while maintaining powerful AI capabilities across desktop environments.

## Editor Interface
The editor supports **Markdown formatting**, allowing users to structure notes with formatted text. The interface is designed to be lightweight and unobtrusive, with the notes window automatically sizing to fit content.

Notes appear as a **floating window above other apps**, accessible via a single hotkey. This design keeps the interface out of the way while remaining instantly available. The window can be opened from the menu bar with a single click or by using ⌥ + click to create a new note.

## Interaction Model
The interface emphasizes **keyboard-first navigation** — users can open, write, and format notes "without touching your mouse." This streamlined approach maintains workflow continuity.

- Meeting notes with action items

- Recently deleted notes can be recovered within 60 days
- Quicklinks enable instant access to favorite notes via hotkeys

The overall design prioritizes speed and minimal friction for capturing thoughts.

# Detail Component Overview

## Core Purpose
The Detail component displays formatted content with optional metadata. It "Renders a markdown (CommonMark) string with an optional metadata panel" and works as either a standalone view or within list navigation flows.

## Main Props

| Prop | Function |
|------|----------|

# Raycast Color System Overview

## Color Enum Values

The standard `Color` enum includes these members:
- **Basic Colors**: Blue, Green, Magenta, Orange, Purple, Red, Yellow
- **Semantic Colors**: PrimaryText, SecondaryText

All standard colors automatically adapt to light and dark themes.

## Dynamic Color API

Dynamic colors accept an object with these properties:

| Property | Type | Required | Purpose |
|----------|------|----------|---------|
| `light` | string | Yes | Color for light theme |
| `dark` | string | Yes | Color for dark theme |
| `adjustContrast` | boolean | No | Enable contrast adjustment (default: true) |

**Example:**
```typescript
tintColor: {
  light: "#FF01FF",
  dark: "#FFFF50",
  adjustContrast: false
}
```

## Color Type Support (ColorLike)

Three color types are accepted wherever colors are used:

1. **Standard Colors** – Built-in enum values with theme adaptation
2. **Dynamic Colors** – Theme-specific hex/RGB/HSL values
3. **Raw Colors** – Simple strings in formats like `"#FF0000"`, `"rgb(255, 0, 0)"`, or `"red"`

Raw and dynamic colors undergo contrast adjustment by default for readability against the Raycast UI, though this can be disabled via the `adjustContrast` property.

An optional array of List.Item.Accessory items are displayed on the right side in a List.Item.

content.content:
# List Component Overview

The Raycast `List` component provides a robust interface for displaying organized content with built-in features.

## Key Features

The component offers several capabilities out of the box:

- **Built-in Filtering**: "Use built-in filtering for best performance" by matching against item titles and keywords
- **Sections**: "Group-related items in sections with titles and subtitles"
- **Loading States**: "Show loading indicator for longer operations"
- **Search Integration**: "Use the search query for typeahead experiences, optionally throttled"

## Search Functionality

The search bar enables quick user interaction. By default, items display if their title or keywords match the user's fuzzy search input.

### Custom Filtering

You can disable native filtering by setting `filtering` to `false` and implement your own logic. Note that specifying an `onSearchTextChange` listener implicitly disables filtering unless you explicitly set `filtering` to `true`.

### Pagination Support

Lists support pagination through a `pagination` prop containing:
- `onLoadMore`: Called when users reach the list end
- `hasMore`: Indicates whether more items exist
- `pageSize`: Number of placeholder items shown during loading

Memory usage increases with pagination, so Raycast monitors extension memory and may prevent further pagination if unsafe.

## Component Structure

Lists can contain `List.Section` components (for grouped items) or direct `List.Item` elements. Sections automatically organize content hierarchically and support titles plus optional subtitles.

- Added a new prop type to Form.DatePicker to control the date components asked from the user
- Added 2 new Actions: Action.CreateSnippet and Action.CreateQuicklink
- Dropdowns and ActionPanel Submenus now support properties like onSearchTextChange, isLoading, throttle, and filtering

- Added the getFrontmostApplication method to get the current frontmost Application of the system

**2024 Updates:**
- Raycast AI was enhanced with Anthropic Claude 3, Perplexity, and more models
- Added the ability to find and replace text in Raycast Notes, as well as the option to adjust note content scale with shortcuts or actions

# Raycast UI Comparison: Compact Mode vs. Normal Mode

## Compact Mode Visual Differences

In Compact Mode, the launcher initially displays only the search bar without the typical list of shortcuts. When users begin typing, "the interface expands to its normal view, i.e., to display the relevant results." This design choice allows users to see content behind the panel before entering a search query.

The standard interface features a larger search bar—described as "almost the same size of Spotlight's search bar"—making it "visually easier to distinguish the bar from the search results."

## Action Bar Design

The Action Bar appears at the bottom of the Raycast window and displays the primary command available for the user's input. It includes a button that serves as a shortcut to access additional actions. When clicked, this button reveals a pop-up panel listing relevant options (such as "opening the file, show in finder, quick look, open with" for file searches).

## Design Details

The article notes that Raycast updated its app icon to resemble a key cap, reflecting its keystroke launcher function. The icon set throughout the application was also redesigned, though the author notes these are subtle changes with no modifications to core functionality.

Raycast's built-in features include: Raycast AI, Raycast Notes, Raycast Focus, Clipboard History, Window Management, Snippets, File Search, Quicklinks, Calculator, Calendar, System, and Emoji Picker.

---

# Raycast Complete UI/UX Map

## Understanding

This is a research-only task. No code changes are needed. Below is a structured, comprehensive map of every screen, view, component, and interaction pattern in Raycast, organized by category. This is sourced from the official developer API docs, the manual, changelogs, and design artifacts.

---

## 1. CORE WINDOW ANATOMY

The Raycast window is a floating panel that appears center-screen (or in a user-configured position). It has two display modes:

### 1.1 Normal Mode (Expanded)
- Full search bar at top (larger than Spotlight-equivalent)
- Result list below the search bar (visible immediately on open)
- Action bar at the bottom

### 1.2 Compact Mode
- Only the search bar is visible on open
- List expands below the search bar only after the user types
- Cleaner, minimal appearance when idle

### 1.3 Window Structural Regions (top to bottom)

```
+-------------------------------------------+
|  [Icon] Search Input                  [v] |   <-- Search Bar + optional Dropdown
+-------------------------------------------+
|  [Section Title]                          |   <-- List Section Header (optional)
|  [Icon] Item Title         [acc][acc][acc]|   <-- List.Item with accessories
|  [Icon] Item Title (selected, highlighted)|
|  ...                                      |
+-------------------------------------------+
|  Navigation Title    |  Action  ⌘K  ⌘↵   |   <-- Action Bar (bottom)
+-------------------------------------------+
```

**Action Bar (bottom panel):**
- Left side: Navigation title (breadcrumb for current view depth) + toast notification area
- Right side: Primary action label + keyboard shortcut + "⌘K" to open ActionPanel

---

## 2. TOP-LEVEL SCREENS / VIEWS

### 2.1 Root Search (Command Palette)
The default view. Shows all installed commands, apps, quicklinks, and recent items as the user types. Uses fuzzy matching across title, subtitle, keywords, and alias. Result list is a `List` view with sectioned items.

### 2.2 List View
Used by extensions to show multiple similar items (e.g. GitHub PRs, files, todos).

- `List` — container with search bar and optional dropdown
- `List.Section` — grouped items with optional title + subtitle
- `List.Item` — single row with: icon, title, optional subtitle, optional accessories (right-aligned)
- `List.EmptyView` — shown when no items match; has optional icon + title + description
- `List.Dropdown` — accessory element in search bar for secondary filtering
- `List.Dropdown.Section` / `List.Dropdown.Item`

**Two-pane (isShowingDetail) layout:** When `isShowingDetail=true`, the list splits into a left pane (list) and a right pane (Detail panel with markdown + metadata). Accessories are hidden in this mode.

### 2.3 Grid View
For media-heavy content (icons, images, colors, emojis). Close to `List` in API shape.

- `Grid` — container with columns (1–8), fit options (Contain/Fill), inset options (Small/Medium/Large)
- `Grid.Section` — grouped items with optional title + subtitle
- `Grid.Item` — cell displaying image/emoji/color, with optional title and tooltip
- `Grid.EmptyView`
- `Grid.Dropdown` / `Grid.Dropdown.Section` / `Grid.Dropdown.Item`

### 2.4 Detail View
For expanded information on a selected item. Used standalone or embedded in List two-pane.

- `Detail` — renders CommonMark markdown in the main area
  - Supports LaTeX (inline `\(...\)` and display `$$...$$`)
  - Supports image sizing via URL query params (`raycast-width`, `raycast-height`, `raycast-tintColor`)
- `Detail.Metadata` — right sidebar with structured data
  - `Detail.Metadata.Label` — key/value pair with optional icon and text color
  - `Detail.Metadata.Link` — clickable link
  - `Detail.Metadata.TagList` — horizontal row of colored tag pills
  - `Detail.Metadata.TagList.Item` — individual tag
  - `Detail.Metadata.Separator` — visual divider line

### 2.5 Form View
For creating or editing data.

- `Form` — container with submit handling and draft preservation
  - `Form.TextField` — single-line text input
  - `Form.PasswordField` — masked text input
  - `Form.TextArea` — multi-line text, optional markdown support
  - `Form.Checkbox` — boolean toggle
  - `Form.Dropdown` — single-select dropdown with optional sections
  - `Form.TagPicker` — multi-select tag input
  - `Form.DatePicker` — date/datetime picker with `min`, `max`, `type` (date vs date+time)
  - `Form.FilePicker` — file/directory picker, single or multiple
  - `Form.Separator` — visual divider
  - `Form.LinkAccessory` — optional link shown in search bar area

### 2.6 Action Panel (Overlay)
Opens via `⌘K`. A modal-like panel showing all available actions for the focused item.

- `ActionPanel` — container
- `ActionPanel.Section` — visually separated group of actions with optional title
- `ActionPanel.Submenu` — nested submenu that replaces the panel with child actions

**Built-in Action types (Action.\*):**
| Action | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `Action` | Generic, custom `onAction` callback |
| `Action.CopyToClipboard` | Copies content, shows HUD confirmation |
| `Action.Paste` | Pastes into front-most application |
| `Action.Open` | Opens file/folder with specific app |
| `Action.OpenInBrowser` | Opens URL in default browser |
| `Action.OpenWith` | Opens file/URL via user-chosen app (submenu) |
| `Action.ShowInFinder` | Reveals file in Finder |
| `Action.Trash` | Moves file to Trash |
| `Action.Push` | Pushes new view onto navigation stack |
| `Action.SubmitForm` | Submits a Form |
| `Action.ToggleQuickLook` | Toggles Quick Look preview |
| `Action.PickDate` | Inline date picker action |
| `Action.CreateSnippet` | Pre-fills and navigates to Create Snippet |
| `Action.CreateQuicklink` | Pre-fills and navigates to Create Quicklink |

Default keyboard shortcuts: `↵` = primary action, `⌘↵` = secondary action (List/Grid/Detail); `⌘↵` = primary, `⌘⇧↵` = secondary (Form).

---

## 3. FEEDBACK / OVERLAY COMPONENTS

### 3.1 Toast
A non-blocking notification strip that appears within the Raycast window.

- Three styles: `Toast.Style.Animated` (spinner, for ongoing ops), `Toast.Style.Success` (checkmark), `Toast.Style.Failure` (X)
- Fields: `title`, `message`, `style`, `primaryAction`, `secondaryAction`
- Actions have: `title`, `onAction(toast)`, optional `shortcut`
- If the window is closed while a Toast is active, it falls back to HUD
- API: `showToast(options)` returns a mutable `Toast` object (can call `.show()`, `.hide()`, update properties dynamically)

### 3.2 Alert / Confirmation Dialog
A blocking modal dialog for destructive or important confirmations.

- Fields: `title`, `message`, `icon`, `primaryAction`, `dismissAction`, `rememberUserChoice`
- `Alert.ActionStyle`: `Default`, `Destructive`, `Cancel`
- API: `confirmAlert(options)` returns `Promise<boolean>`

### 3.3 HUD (Heads-Up Display)
A compact floating message shown at the bottom-center of the screen after Raycast closes.

- Used for confirming clipboard copies, actions that require hiding Raycast
- Auto-dismisses after a short delay
- API: `showHUD(title, options?)`

---

## 4. NAVIGATION PATTERNS

### 4.1 Stack-Based Navigation
Raycast uses a push/pop navigation stack:

- `useNavigation()` hook returns `{ push, pop }`
- `push(component, onPop?)` — pushes a new React component as a full-screen view
- `pop()` — goes back to previous view; also triggered by `ESC` key
- `popToRoot()` — collapses the entire navigation stack back to root search
- `Action.Push` — declarative way to push a view via an action

### 4.2 Navigation Header
When inside a pushed view (non-root), the action bar left-side shows the `navigationTitle` prop as a breadcrumb. `⌘ESC` always returns to root.

### 4.3 Global Keyboard Navigation
| Shortcut | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `ESC` | Pop to previous view |
| `⌘ESC` | Return to root search |
| `⌘W` | Dismiss Raycast window entirely |
| `⌘,` | Open Preferences |
| `⌃N` / `⌃P` | Move down/up in lists |
| `⌘K` | Open Action Panel |
| `⌘⇧F` | Toggle item as favorite |
| `⌘⌥↑`/`⌘⌥↓` | Reorder favorites |
| `⌘⇧,` | Open selected item's preferences |

---

## 5. BUILT-IN CORE SCREENS AND COMMANDS

These are Raycast's native extensions (not third-party), each represented as a command in root search:

### 5.1 Clipboard History
- List view of all copied items (text, images, colors, links)
- Searchable, filterable by type
- Supports pinned entries
- "Paste Sequentially" command
- Text recognition from copied images

### 5.2 Snippet Manager
- List view of all snippets
- Fuzzy-searchable by name, keyword, content
- Trigger keyword inline expansion from any text field in any app
- Supports Dynamic Placeholders: `{clipboard}`, `{date}`, `{time}`, `{uuid}`, `{selection}`, `{cursor}`, `{argument}`, `{snippet name="..."}`
- Create Snippet: Form view (name, keyword, body, dynamic placeholders)

### 5.3 Quicklinks
- List view of saved quicklinks (URLs, file paths, deep links, web searches)
- Dynamic placeholders support same tokens as snippets
- Create Quicklink: Form view
- Quicklinks appear directly in root search

### 5.4 File Search
- List view with file/folder results
- Quick Look preview via `Action.ToggleQuickLook`
- "Show in Finder", "Open With" actions
- Filter by file type

### 5.5 Calculator
- Inline in root search — no separate screen
- User types a math expression, result appears immediately below search bar
- `Enter` copies result to clipboard

### 5.6 Emoji Picker
- Grid view of all emojis, searchable
- Type `:` in any text field to trigger inline
- Or search "Emoji Picker" command directly

### 5.7 Window Management
- No visual UI — pure keyboard shortcut dispatcher
- Commands: Left Half, Right Half, Top Half, Bottom Half, Top Left/Right Quarter, Bottom Left/Right Quarter, Center, Maximize, Fullscreen, First/Last Third, First/Last Two-Thirds, etc.
- Assign global hotkeys per command in Preferences

### 5.8 AI Chat
- Standalone chat window (separate from main Raycast window)
- Multi-turn conversation interface
- 32+ AI models (OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, Perplexity, Google, etc.)
- Model switcher picker within chat
- Custom presets (model + system prompt)
- File/PDF/CSV attachment support
- Mid-conversation model comparison (regenerate with different model)
- Web search toggle for real-time info with inline citations
- `⌘K` applies AI commands to previous messages

### 5.9 Quick AI
- Floating overlay above other apps
- Single-question interface (not persistent chat)
- Triggered by dedicated global hotkey
- Keyboard-driven, dismisses after answer

### 5.10 Notes (formerly Floating Notes)
- Floating window above other apps
- Markdown editor with rich formatting: headings, bold/italic/strikethrough, inline code, code blocks, lists, checklists, links
- Multiple notes support
- Cloud sync across Macs
- Export: plain text, markdown, HTML, Apple Notes
- Accessible from menu bar with `⌥+click` to create new note

### 5.11 Store (Extension Store)
- Searchable list of all extensions
- "Show Details" action navigates to extension detail screen
- Extension detail: command list, developer info, screenshots carousel, install/open action
- Category filtering
- Inline installation flow; terms acceptance on first open

### 5.12 System Commands
Quick-toggle list commands including: Lock Screen, Sleep, Restart, Shut Down, Toggle System Appearance (Dark/Light), Adjust Volume/Brightness, Empty Trash, Toggle Hidden Files, Toggle Do Not Disturb, Bluetooth toggle, etc.

---

## 6. SETTINGS / PREFERENCES SCREENS

Opened via `⌘,` or search "Preferences". A native settings panel with tabs:

| Tab | Contents |
|---|---|
| **General** | Launch at login, replace Spotlight (set hotkey), root search sensitivity (High/Low), window position, pop-to-root timing |
| **Appearance** | Theme (Light/Dark/System), text size options |
| **Extensions** | Enable/disable extensions and individual commands; aliases; hotkeys; right panel for extension-specific settings (e.g. API tokens, configurations) |
| **Advanced** | Display positioning, export/import preferences |
| **About** | Version info, website link, feedback, acknowledgements |

---

## 7. MENU BAR COMMANDS

A distinct command type where extensions live in the macOS system menu bar (not in the Raycast search window).

- `MenuBarExtra` — root container; displays icon and/or text in menu bar
- `MenuBarExtra.Item` — clickable menu entry with optional icon, subtitle
- `MenuBarExtra.Submenu` — nested group that expands on hover
- `MenuBarExtra.Section` — separator-delimited group with optional title
- Commands can auto-refresh on an `interval` schedule
- Activated by: direct click, scheduled refresh, Raycast restart, or re-enabling in preferences
- Returns `null` to remove itself from the menu bar

---

## 8. LIST ITEM ACCESSORY TYPES

Items on the right side of a `List.Item`, rendered as a row of metadata. Each accessory has an optional `tooltip`.

| Accessory Type | Visual |
|---|---|
| `{ text: string, color?: Color }` | Colored or default text label |
| `{ icon: Image.ImageLike }` | Small icon (16–18px) |
| `{ date: Date }` | Relative or absolute date string |
| `{ tag: { value: string, color?: Color } }` | Pill badge with optional color |
| Any combination of the above with `tooltip` | Shown on hover |

---

## 9. ICON SYSTEM

`Icon` enum covers ~150+ named outline-style icons with consistent stroke widths:

Categories include: Arrows (ArrowUp/Down/Left/Right, ChevronUp/Down), UI controls (Check, Circle, Dot, Ellipsis), Actions (Copy, Paste, Download, Upload, Delete, Duplicate), Objects (Document, Folder, Calendar, Clock, Gear), Status (Battery, Signal, Cloud, Bolt), Social/Content (Star, Heart, Gift, Flag, Lock, Key), and many more.

Color system:
- Built-in semantic colors (auto-adapt to theme): `Blue`, `Green`, `Magenta`, `Orange`, `Purple`, `Red`, `Yellow`, `PrimaryText`, `SecondaryText`
- Dynamic colors: `{ light: "#hex", dark: "#hex", adjustContrast?: boolean }`
- Raw strings: `"#F00"`, `"rgb(255,0,0)"`, `"hsla(200,20%,33%,0.2)"`

---

## 10. WINDOW & SEARCH BAR API

Programmatic control of the Raycast window:

- `clearSearchBar({ forceScrollToTop?: boolean })` — clears the search input text
- `closeMainWindow({ clearRootSearch?: boolean, popToRootType?: PopToRootType })` — hides Raycast
- `popToRoot({ clearSearchBar?: boolean })` — collapses nav stack to root

---

## 11. DYNAMIC PLACEHOLDER SYSTEM

Used in Quicklinks, Snippets, and AI Commands:

| Placeholder | Value |
|---|---|
| `{clipboard}` | Last copied text |
| `{selection}` | Selected text in frontmost app |
| `{cursor}` | Cursor position after paste |
| `{date}` | Current date |
| `{time}` | Current time |
| `{datetime}` | Date + time |
| `{day}` | Day of week |
| `{uuid}` | New UUID |
| `{argument}` | User input prompt |
| `{snippet name="..."}` | Referenced snippet content |
| `{browser-tab}` | Current browser tab (URL, title, markdown, HTML) |

Modifiers: `uppercase`, `lowercase`, `trim`, `percent-encode`, `json-stringify`, date formatting via `{date format="yyyy-MM-dd"}`.

---

## 12. KEY VISUAL DESIGN FACTS

- **Window shape**: Floating panel, centered on screen (or user-configured). No native title bar. Semi-transparent/blurred background (glassmorphism-adjacent) in some themes.
- **Search bar**: Full-width at top, large (comparable to Spotlight), focused by default. Contains optional dropdown accessory (right side).
- **List items**: Icon (left) + Title + optional Subtitle + Accessories (right-aligned). Selected item has a highlighted background.
- **Action bar**: Fixed at window bottom. Left: navigation breadcrumb + toast area. Right: primary action text + shortcut badge + "⌘K" pill.
- **Icon style**: Outline style with bold stroke width; consistent corner radii. Available as `Icon.*` enum.
- **Color scheme**: Full dark and light theme support. System-default follows macOS appearance.
- **Typography**: Two text size options in Preferences (Appearance tab).
- **Sections**: Have a title row in a de-emphasized style above their items.
- **EmptyView**: Centered icon + title + optional description text. Appears when filtering yields no results.
- **Toast**: Appears in the action bar left area (not a separate popup); animated spinner / checkmark / X variants.
- **Alert**: Full blocking modal dialog overlaid on Raycast window.
- **HUD**: Small pill/bar at screen bottom, appears after Raycast window closes.
- **Quick Look**: macOS native Quick Look overlay triggered for file previews.
- **App icon**: Key-cap design (keyboard key metaphor).
